steps.title: Track the logic
steps.content: The text sets up a tempting question (how would a handwritten novel differ if typed?) and then blocks it: "each novel is a unique entity resulting from a specific set of circumstances." If each work is one-of-a-kind, there is no counterfactual version to compare it against.

steps.title: Predict the completion
steps.content: "Therefore" demands a conclusion that follows from uniqueness: you cannot meaningfully judge how the novel would have turned out by other means.

steps.title: Eliminate the others
steps.content:
• Choice A — in order to increase their efficiency, authors who currently write their novels largely by hand should instead work only on a computer.: prescribes a behavior change the passage never argues for; overreach. ✗
  • Choice B — authors who do most of their drafting and revising by hand likely have more success than those who work entirely on a computer.: claims handwriting yields better novels, which the passage never supports; overreach. ✗
  • Choice C — novels written by hand take less time to produce, on average, than novels written on a computer do.: introduces a speed comparison absent from the passage; misread. ✗
  • Choice D — there is no way to reasonably evaluate how a work would be different if it had been written by other means.: follows directly from each novel being a unique product of its circumstances. ✓
That matches Choice D — there is no way to reasonably evaluate how a work would be different if it had been written by other means..
